content.full_text:
November 14, 1985


Leo Burke, Member
California Veterans Board
c/o 1727 No. San Joaquin Street
Stockton, CA 95204

Re: Your Request for Advice;
Our File No. A-85-224

Dear Mr. Burke:

You have written requesting our advice as to whether there
is any conflict of interest inherent in your situation, which
you have described as follows:

FACTS

You have served and continue to serve, without any form of
compensation, as the volunteer treasurer for the committee
formed to support the passage of Proposition 29 on the 1984
ballot (the Veterans Bond Act of 1984). The committee (I.D.
No. 841835) is still actively seeking and receiving
contributions to pay expenses incurred in the successful
campaign.

You have recently been appointed to the California Veterans
Board, a policy-making body affiliated with the California
Department of Veterans Affairs, which is the agency that
administers the funds resulting from the sale of the bonds
which were approved via the ballot measure.

CONCLUSION

There is no conflict of interest under the Political Reform
Act1/ presented by your situation. The conflict of interest

_________________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. Commission
advice is limited to the provisions of the Political Reform Act.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Mr. Leo Burke
November 14, 1985
Page 2


provisions of the Act deal with conflicts between economic
interests and official duties. Your political interests are
not a basis for a conflict of interest under the Act.
Obviously, you may not use state resources for your work as a
treasurer, or any other personal matters, but that is something
I am sure you were aware of anyway.

I trust this letter adequately responds to your question.
Should you have any questions regarding this letter, I may be
reached at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Robert E. Ledigh
Counsel
Legal Division

REL:nwm

STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN, Governor

CALIFORNIA VETERANS BOARD
POST OFFICE BOX 1559, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95807
                                                                Oct 31  9 06 AH 05

October 29, 1985


Ms. Barbara Milman, General Counsel
California Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re:  1984  Proposition 29 Ballot Measure - Veterans Bond Act

Dear Ms. Milman:

An acquaintance of mine recently had a discussion with
John Keplinger relative to my term as treasurer of the
1984 Proposition 29 ballot measure (that was the Veterans
Bond Act of 1984). From the time of the inception of the
committee supporting Proposition 29 (pac# 841835) to the
present time, I have served as treasurer and have tried
faithfully to fulfill the duties of that office. For your
record I have recently moved from 2132 Oregon Avenue,
Stockton, CA to 1727 No. San Joaquin Street, Stockton, CA
95204.

Recently, I was appointed by the Governor as a member of
the California Veterans Board, a unit of the California
Department of Veterans Affairs. My question is this: is
there any conflict of interest or other reason why I should
not fulfill both roles, ie, member of the California Veterans
Board and remain treasurer of the Proposition 29 ballot
measure? For your information, the Proposition passed and
the Commission is still seeking and receiving the additional
funds required to pay expenses incurred.

I would appreciate it very much if you would let me have
your opinion on this matter. I will very happily follow
any advice you can give.

Sincerely,

LEO BURKE, Member
California Veterans Board

cc:  Grover McKean, Rothschild, Unterberg, Towdin
     Director, Department of Veterans Affairs


HIRE A VETERAN – HIRE EXPERIENCE
